replies.author: @shobhanparthi
replies.text: Actually i use the same way as it was explained but the motor doesnt spins.... i used 2 HDD motors 1) Seagate ST340810A and 2) HP DG0300FARVV, the first one has 5400RPM 2.5Amps 7.5W 5V to 12V and the second one with 12v 0.4A to +5v 0.6A. with the first one i had a power supply of 12v 0.5Amp so i thought its the amperes where i am lagging behind and later with second one, which suppose to spin as per its data sheet doesnt worked as desired. So i am seeking help to understand why its not spinning. further i am using a ESC of 30Amp. I kindly request you to please help me out in this please !! i am new beginner so i desperately need help on it. Or if you can suggest a forum where i can get some responses over it .... Please !!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ok - it's got noting to do with the amps or volts - there is a min and max in which the motors will operate but they will operate down to surprising low volts and amps. These are DC brushless motors so it has to do with how you go about shifting the phase of the input to create a rotating magnetic field within the coils. You can do this with an arduino driver but the easiest way is with a capacitor with larger motors you can use an ac supply - as you don't really understand this i suspect you need to do a bit of background reading on these motors first

author: @CoherentPhoton
text: Nice video. I have played around with a few motors from different brands of hard drive and they are all pretty similar but also they only run really well in one direction. I think this is because the fluid dynamic bearing in them is designed that way. You can see the effect quite clearly by just spinning it with your hand.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
thanks that is awesome, I was just tearing a bunch of them up *(HDDs) for the kidney magnets and saving the motors and boards and then testing each with the molex connector from an ATX power supply to see which did what I wanted of running till I unhooked it lol, found that about 1 in about every 8 I had was willing to do just that, rest would do either nothing or would run for a bit then shut down (probably some feed back it was needing from the heads and such that it was not getting lol)

Now we can make mini centrifuges XD

author: @DigGil3
text:
Actually I have sort of a pet peeve with using ready-to-plug modules like that. Because, you know... you end up not actually understanding what's happening  in the "black box" :P.
The signal generator could be replaced by a any oscillator, right? One could use a 555 Timer if you are familiar with those. Or a PWM capable microcontroller if you have these. Or a quartz oscillator as in the old days. But personally I have many OpAmps lying around, so I would build it using a Schmitt Trigger Oscillator (http://sound.westhost.com/dwop-f11.gif) Where R2 and R3 could be replaced with a single potentiometer so you could adjust speed (that is, frequency).

I've been considering building my own stepper motor driver for a while ever since I've learnt about quadrature oscillators (http://www.techlib.com/electronics/graphics/stepper3.gif). These are circuits which have two or three phase shifting elements (in this case, opamps as mathematical integrators) feeding back to each other such that they can't settle on a voltage. Each element will output a frequency which is out of phase to the next. This allows the motors to run (preferably through a power amplifier of sorts) without any external signal generator.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: DigGil3 Sure and that's good - but not everyone wants to understand they just want to do - there are some very good reasons for this - it takes a very very long time to understand every aspect of everything and a particular aspect may just be unimportant to your main idea - then you just want a component that does it's job so you can get one looking at whatever it is you are investigating as prime

text: This was a nice and short one, just do not cut the ground wire when you will use two powersupplys, you will need a common ground between those powersupplys, and to supply a ground to the signal too. just think of it as ion bridge for an electrolyte in a battery ;) nice work, I like the graphene videos most, but as an information addict, I like anything that brings scientific methods to non technobablic form.
